content.assessment: This proposal accepts network contraction as the default and shifts the lens question toward whether transport and equity mitigation can adequately substitute for lost geographic access; the evidence on both transport-responsibility clarity and closure-population effects remains unsettled, limiting confidence in the mitigation's sufficiency.
opportunities: Existing free public transport for under-14s provides partial infrastructure to build statutory transport guarantees upon, though it does not resolve frequency limitations in the highest-depopulation regions
strengths: Directly addresses the transport criterion by proposing statutory clarity where legal responsibility for pupil transport is currently disputed and unassigned
Targets capacity and equity together by tying anti-segregation safeguards to closure decisions in a context of documented Roma enrollment disparities tied to settlement geography
threats: Closure-population decline causal link is genuinely contested internationally (Finnish DID studies vs. Saxony/Sweden null findings; only 17% of residents rating school absence a major shortcoming), meaning the policy's core justification for defaulting to closure with mitigation rests on unsettled evidence

Informal inter-municipal competition and settlement-level Roma enrollment disparities suggest mitigation focused on individual pupil transport may not address network-level segregation effects from closures
weaknesses: Even robust transport guarantees do not resolve the underlying rural transport frequency and scheduling gaps documented across North-Eastern, South-Western, and Western regions, meaning statutory entitlement may not translate into practical access
Consolidation cost savings themselves are contested (Texas vs. NY findings), weakening the fiscal rationale underlying threshold-triggered defaults toward closure
